name: datetime.utcnow() naive timestamp inserted into tz-aware schema
description: datetime.utcnow() is deprecated since Python 3.12 and returns a naive datetime with no tzinfo. When the rest of the codebase uses datetime.now(timezone.utc), inserting a naive timestamp causes subtle schema inconsistencies: ORM parsers fail to compare values, JSON serializers produce different string formats (no +00:00 suffix), and time-windowed queries produce wrong results.

bad_example.code:
def backfill_record(cur, mem_id, content, importance, source, bank):
    now = datetime.utcnow().isoformat()
    cur.execute(
        "INSERT INTO memories (id, content, importance, source, created_at, bank)"
        " VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?)",
        (mem_id, content[:4000], importance, source, now, bank)
    )
bad_example.why_bad: datetime.utcnow() is deprecated (3.12+) AND returns a naive datetime. .isoformat() on a naive datetime produces '2026-06-03T12:00:00' (no tz suffix). The ORM elsewhere uses datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat() which produces '2026-06-03T12:00:00+00:00'. Mixed timestamps break ORDER BY, range queries, and any ORM that checks tzinfo before parsing.

good_example.code:
from datetime import datetime, timezone

def backfill_record(cur, mem_id, content, importance, source, bank):
    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()
    cur.execute(
        "INSERT INTO memories (id, content, importance, source, created_at, bank)"
        " VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ?)",
        (mem_id, content[:4000], importance, source, now, bank)
    )
good_example.why_better: datetime.now(timezone.utc) is the modern replacement. Produces tz-aware datetime with +00:00 suffix in isoformat(), consistent with the rest of the codebase.
